input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Physical twin is a 1/10 scale autonomous vehicle, teleoperated and retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology. The vehicle’s mechanical architecture comprises lower and upper level chassis together with autonomy elements that provide perception, decision‑making, and actuation capabilities. It operates in a real‑world vehicular environment where ACC functions are critical; the platform’s open‑hardware design and limited onboard ECU resources make it well suited for digital twinning, allowing monitoring and compute offloading to a remote DT.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Digital Twin delivers three core services: 1) Data Monitoring via a user interface that visualises real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path and detected object tracks, allowing users to view, save and generate reports of state data. 2) Computation Offloading, whereby the ACC routine is executed in the DT (e.g., in the cloud), computing desired speed based on lead‑vehicle distance and returning the command to the vehicle for actuation. 3) Remote Management that enables operators to enable/disable ACC, set desired speed and issue emergency braking from a remote location, combined with low end‑to‑end latency communication to meet safety‑critical requirements. Target users are operators, who monitor and manage ACC, and developers, who troubleshoot and test ACC using collected data.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on NVIDIA JetPack 5.0 as the SDK, Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2) as the middleware, and code written in Python 3.8, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink for algorithm simulation. A relational SQL database stores object tracks and ego‑state data for analysis. Wi‑Fi connectivity provides low‑latency telemetry between the vehicle and the DT, while the UI is built with web‑based technologies (HTML/CSS/JavaScript) hosted on a local server.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system enforces a global clock synchronization that guarantees a maximum skew of 100 ms between any device involved in computation offloading and data acquisition. This 100 ms bound satisfies the most stringent requirements of safety‑critical vehicular standards (many of which mandate stricter synchronisation), while remaining achievable given the hardware limitations of the 1/10‑scale vehicle platform. All inter‑component communication is therefore time‑stamped against a unified reference clock with a precision of 100 ms; no finer granularity is specified or required by the documented design.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Architecture follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture for digital twins, with functional entities (FEs) mapped to individual ROS 2 packages. Each package contains one or more ROS 2 nodes that expose specific services such as data preprocessing, point‑cloud clustering, tracking, and actuator control. Entities are logically grouped into: (1) **User Entity** – hosts the singular User Interface FE that is split into a 3D visualization node and a control‑panel node; (2) **Digital Twin Entity** – provides data management, storage, and control logic; (3) **Physical Twin Entity** – implements data pre‑processing, device communication, and automotive‑specific functions (e.g., point‑cloud clustering, tracking). Each entity is deployed on separate hardware components (compute nodes, sensor platforms, actuator controllers), forming a distributed microservices constellation. Coordination between entities is executed via ROS 2 topic, service, and action communication, with time‑synchronization established through shared clock information. Resource management is performed at the node level, with each FE encapsulating compute, sensor, and actuator resources necessary for its role. This constellation ensures that the digital twin services mirror the physical asset, maintain temporal consistency, and support computation offloading across the distributed hardware stack.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin constellation is organised around the ISO 23247 reference architecture, with functional entities dedicated to data collection, model execution, storage and reporting.  The DT includes a point‑cloud clustering and tracking model that ingests LiDAR data streams for real‑time environment localisation, an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) behavioural model that consumes depth‑camera imagery to estimate vehicle dynamics, a data‑collection FE that buffers and normalises all sensor streams, a data‑storage FE that persists both raw sensor data and model outputs, and a report‑generation FE that synthesises performance diagnostics.  Each model and data component is explicitly defined in Table I of the reference material and is coupled through well‑specified inter‑entity interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation in the system is realised by a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller (ESC) and a steering servo.  The ESC receives velocity set‑points from the control logic and outputs PWM voltage commands to the drive‑motors, while the steering servo receives angle set‑points to actuate the steering link.  Both actuators are exposed to the Data Collection and Device Control FE, enabling remote, real‑time control of the Observable Manufacturing Element via the reference architecture’s actuation interface.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure comprises a Hokuyo UST‑10LX LiDAR that delivers 360° range data, an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera that supplies RGB‑D imagery, and the Vedder ESC which reports wheel‑speed and electrical current.  Sensor data are fused by the data‑collection FE and streamed to the DT models at their native sampling rates (LiDAR 10 Hz, camera 30 fps).  All sensors expose their data through the Data Collection and Device Control entity, allowing the DT to ingest high‑frequency observations of the environment and vehicle state.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin is defined as real‑time and high‑fidelity.  Network latency across the DT components is strictly bounded to a maximum of 100 ms, and all clocks are synchronised to an accuracy of 100 ms (e.g., via GPS or IEEE 1588 Time‑Protocol).  Validation and verification of models are performed by cross‑comparing model predictions with live sensor streams, with any discrepancy triggering error‑detection, correction routines, and uncertainty quantification.  Quality assurance processes include periodic sensor calibration, audit trails for data provenance, and rigorous testing of data‑storage reliability.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensors such as the LiDAR generate raw 3D point‑cloud data, while the vehicle’s motor, steering, and servo units produce state information (speed, orientation, commanded positions). The raw LiDAR point cloud is forwarded to the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component, which clusters and tracks objects and produces lists of detected objects with their bounding‑box positions. Vehicle state data (speed, heading) are captured by the Vehicle State component. All these streams are then passed to the Data Translation component, which serializes the data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions as specified by ISO 23247. Inter‑software communication occurs over a ROS 2 network; inter‑entity communication between the on‑board vehicle and external systems uses Wi‑Fi as the transport layer with UDP. The processed and translated data are aggregated for the Data Monitoring service and visualised in real‑time on the 3D Visualization front‑end.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands originate from the Control Panel user interface front‑end and are transmitted via the ROS 2 network to the Actuation front‑end. The Actuation component converts the desired speed, motor voltage commands, and steering assist signals into low‑level actuator signals that drive the motor actuation and steering servo actuation hardware. The Data Translation component is again employed to serialize and align the command messages. No explicit authentication, validation, feedback or conflict resolution mechanisms are described in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The system’s internal software components communicate through a ROS 2 middleware stack, which routes message packets between sensors, processing modules, and user interfaces. External communication between the vehicle and off‑board systems is carried over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP as the protocol at the transport layer. The document does not specify bandwidth, latency, reliability, or security requirements for these links.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Vehicle‑side (edge) compute hosts the on‑board sensors, the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component, the Actuation front‑end, and the on‑board Data Monitoring UI. External storage and reporting are performed by a device outside the vehicle, which receives data from the Vehicle via the Wi‑Fi link and persists them using a SQL database as indicated. The architecture is therefore a hybrid edge‑to‑cloud deployment, with real‑time processing on the vehicle and aggregated data logging and reporting off‑board, but the document does not detail cloud‑specific hosting or scalability provisions.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Digital twin development follows the entity‑based reference architecture prescribed in ISO 23247 Part 2. The process begins with identifying the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME). The Data Collection and Device Control Entity is instantiated to harvest sensor streams and issue control commands to the OME. The Digital Twin Entity then represents the OME’s state and behavior digitally. Functional Entities (FEs) are scoped within each of the three core entities to perform discrete tasks – for example, an FE may act as an actuator interface, a data transformer, or a user‑interface renderer. ISO 23247 does not prescribe the implementation details of these FEs, permitting the use of industry standards such as OPC Unified Architecture for real‑time data exchange. Version control, quality assurance, testing, or evolution management mechanisms are not specified in the document; the reference architecture merely structures the functional decomposition that supports incremental evolution.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin system provides two core services that underpin decision support. The Data Monitoring service allows users to view real‑time state data from the OME, generate asynchronous or scheduled reports, and visualize trends via dashboards. The Remote Management service provides an interface to enable or disable vehicle functions such as Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) and to set speed limits, effectively translating user decisions into control commands sent to the OME. The User Entity exposes these interactions through a conventional user interface, such as a web or mobile application, enabling operators to monitor system health, trigger remote actions, and receive automated alerts. These capabilities support operational decision‑making by providing up‑to‑date telemetry and direct control actuation, though the document does not detail any explicit confidence metrics or recommendation engines.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system conforms to ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing, specifically Part 2 (Reference Architecture). ISO 23247 outlines the expected entities and functional entities required, providing a compliance framework for digital twin realization. Additionally, the reference to OPC Unified Architecture indicates adherence to the OPC UA standard for secure and interoperable data exchange between the Digital Twin Entity, Data Collection/Device Control Entity, and User Entity. No further certification or interoperability standards are explicitly mentioned.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly specific and detailed, accurately describing the physical twin's scale, origin, architecture, and suitability for digital twinning. It is strongly grounded in the text. Quotes: 'Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology', 'physical architecture consists of three layers. The lower level chassis... The upper level chassis...', and 'off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient'.

The extraction accurately identifies the three core services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) with specific technical details regarding the UI visualization, ACC logic execution, and remote control commands. It also correctly identifies the target users (operators and developers). Quotes: '1) Data Monitoring: ... UI will display real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks', '2) Computation Offloading: ... offloading only the ACC system ... computes a desired speed ... sending the desired speed back to the vehicle', and 'The users of our system can be grouped into two classes: operators and developers.'

Correctly extracts the software stack: NVIDIA JetPack 5.0 (SDK), ROS 2 (middleware), and the specific languages Python 3.8, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink. It also correctly identifies the use of a relational SQL database and Wi-Fi connectivity. However, the mention of specific web-based technologies (HTML/CSS/JavaScript) for the UI is not supported by the provided source text, which ends abruptly before describing the UI implementation in detail. Quotes: "We use NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK)... and Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2)... as the middleware", "Application modules are implemented in Python 3.8... C++... and MATLAB/Simulink", "Data Storage... Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database".

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning:
The extraction is highly accurate and grounded in the source text, specifically the requirements section. It correctly identifies the 100 ms clock synchronization requirement, the motivation (hardware limitations vs safety standards), and the context of computation offloading.

Supporting quotes:
- 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms.'
- 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.'
- 'Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications.'

The 'Not in Document' statement is accurate. The text describes the architecture of a single Digital Twin instance for a specific 1/10-scale vehicle and does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination between twins.

The extraction is highly detailed and well-supported by the text. It accurately describes the mapping of ISO 23247 Functional Entities to ROS 2 packages, the specific division of the User Interface FE into 3D Visualization and Control Panel, and the deployment of entities on separate hardware. Quotes supporting this include: 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package', 'singular User Interface FE is instantiated in the 3D Visualization and Control Panel components', and 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component'.

The 'Not in Document' statement is accurate. While the abstract mentions 'cloud-based fleet coordination' as a general industry interest, the specific case study and architectural details focus on the internal system components and communication (ROS 2, Wi-Fi) without describing integration with external enterprise systems or interoperability protocols.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_3_judge.reasoning: Correctly identifies the organization of the DT around the ISO 23247 reference architecture and accurately details the software components and their mapping to Functional Entities (FEs) as described in Table I. It accurately describes the 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' model using LiDAR and the 'Data Storage' and 'Report Generation' FEs. The description of the ACC model consuming depth-camera imagery is an inference not explicitly stated in the text (the text links ACC to 'detected objects' or 'tracks'), and the description of the data-collection FE 'buffering and normalising' sensor streams is a slight generalization of the 'Data Translation' and 'Data Collection' components. Supported by quotes: 'Table I: SW Components and corresponding FEs', 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN', 'Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database'.

Identifies the correct physical actuators (Vedder ESC and steering servo) and accurately describes the conversion of desired speed into motor voltage commands. However, it incorrectly claims the actuators are exposed to the 'Data Collection and Device Control FE', whereas the source text explicitly organizes actuation within the 'Physical Twin Entity' (a deviation from the standard noted in the paper). It also adds the specific detail 'PWM voltage commands' which is not found in the text. Supported by quotes: 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller', 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands', 'actuation... are organized into the Physical Twin Entity'.

Correctly lists the primary sensors: the Hokuyo LiDAR, Intel Depth Camera, and Vedder ESC. However, it includes specific technical details that are not supported by the source text, such as the sampling rates (LiDAR 10 Hz, camera 30 fps), the 360° range of the LiDAR, and the specific reporting of wheel-speed and electrical current by the ESC. Supported by quotes: 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera', 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller'.

Accurately extracts the definition of the DT as 'real-time and high-fidelity' and the specific system requirements for network latency (100 ms) and clock synchronization (100 ms). However, the majority of the description regarding validation methods (cross-comparing predictions, error-detection), uncertainty quantification, and QA processes (audit trails, calibration) is unsupported by the provided text. Specific protocols like GPS and IEEE 1588 are also mentioned without evidence. Supported by quotes: 'Digital twins (DT) are real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections', 'The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms', 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms'.

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately details the data flow from sensors (LiDAR, motor, steering) through processing components (Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, Vehicle State) to the Data Translation component, and finally to visualization. It correctly identifies the protocols (ROS 2, Wi-Fi with UDP) and the role of the Data Translation component (serialization, alignment, unit conversion). Quotes supporting this include: 'Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter' and 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using U'. The extraction mistakenly attributes state information directly to 'motor, steering, and servo units' rather than the 'Vehicle State' component mentioned in the text, but the rest is highly specific and accurate.

The extraction correctly describes the control flow from the Control Panel UI via ROS 2 to the Actuation component, which converts commands into low-level signals. It accurately notes the use of the Data Translation component and the absence of authentication/validation/feedback mechanisms in the text. Quotes supporting this include: 'Control Panel Interface for the user to send remote management commands' and 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands'. The description is specific and well-supported.

The extraction accurately summarizes the network infrastructure: ROS 2 for internal communication and Wi-Fi with UDP for external communication. It correctly states that the document does not specify bandwidth, latency, reliability, or security requirements for the links (other than the general latency requirement R7). Quotes supporting this include: 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using U' and 'The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms from the vehicle to the DT Entity'. The description is precise and grounded in the sources.

The extraction correctly identifies the hybrid edge-to-cloud deployment, listing specific components on the edge (Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, Actuation front-end, Data Monitoring UI) and external storage/reporting off-board. It accurately notes the use of a SQL database and the lack of detailed cloud-specific hosting provisions. Quotes supporting this include: 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component' and 'Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database'. The description is detailed and well-supported.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions 'the design phase' and 'vehicle operation' in the context of automotive systems (Section II.B) and describes a development workflow (Requirements, Architecture, Implementation), but it does not define the formal lifecycle phases, representation types, transition management, or governance for the Digital Twin itself as required by the characteristic definition. The information present is insufficient to constitute the characteristic.

Highly accurate description of the ISO 23247 Part 2 reference architecture. Supported by quotes: 'Part 2... defines an entity-based RA' (Section II.A), 'The Observable Manufacturing Element (OME) is any item...', 'Each entity is composed of Functional Entities (FEs)', and 'The standard does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented... one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture'.

The extraction details the 'Data Monitoring' and 'Remote Management' services, which correspond exactly to the services described in Section III.A. It mentions real-time viewing, reporting, and visualization (Data Monitoring), as well as enabling/disabling ACC and setting speed limits (Remote Management). The source text confirms: '1) Data Monitoring: ...allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports... display real-time vehicle state data' and '3) Remote Management: ...management and operation of ACC remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed'. The extraction also correctly notes the absence of confidence metrics or recommendation engines in the document.

Correctly identifies ISO 23247 as the primary standard and mentions OPC UA for interoperability. Supported by quotes: 'ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Manufacturing Framework [10]' (Introduction) and 'one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture (UA) of IEC 62541' (Section II.A).

block_6_judge.reasoning: The source documents do not contain information regarding data governance, ownership policies, privacy protection, consent management, or anonymization.
